title: “Me, Myself, and I”: The Increasing Narcissism of Western Music
gist: A PLOS One study finds self-focused pronouns ('me,' 'myself,' 'I') have steadily risen in top US and German pop lyrics from 1970-2019 while staying flat in Japan and Hong Kong, suggesting pop lyrics track a Western drift toward individualism.

insights.claim: Use of self-focused pronouns rose steadily in top-10 US and German pop songs from 1970 to 2019 but stayed relatively flat in Japan and Hong Kong, per a PLOS One study led by Marius Golubickis.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Quantifies a long-run Western cultural shift toward individualism; cultural-trend signal more than market.
insights.quote: songs from the Western countries showed a steady increase in the use of self-focused language
insights.locator: Over the past half century, the use of personal pronouns

insights.claim: Pop-music lyrics may be a 'canary in the coal mine' for the broader trend toward individualism driven by social media, algorithmic feeds, and the death of monoculture.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Frames fragmenting niche fandoms as a measurable, accelerating cultural force relevant to media and platform dynamics.
insights.quote: pop-music lyrics could be the canary in the coal mine for this trend toward individualism
insights.locator: According to a new study published in PLOS One
